TypeScript Setup & Compilation

Master the environment to unlock static typing in modern web development.

Welcome to AWD. Today we establish the foundation for TypeScript development by setting up our local environment and understanding the compilation lifecycle.

Agenda

  • TypeScript Overview
  • Node.js Requirements
  • NPM vs NPX
  • Project Initialization
  • The TSC Compiler
  • Configuring tsconfig.json
  • The Transpilation Workflow
We will cover everything from installing Node to running your first compiled JavaScript file.

What is TypeScript?

  • Microsoft-developed open-source language
  • Superset of JavaScript
  • Enforces standards via static typing
  • High adoption in modern industry
TypeScript adds a layer of safety to JavaScript. It is a superset, meaning all valid JS is valid TS.

The Execution Problem

  • Browsers cannot run .ts files
  • Node.js cannot run .ts directly
  • Requires conversion to JavaScript
  • Uses the V8 (Chromium) engine
Because engines only speak JavaScript, we need a middle step to translate our code before execution.

Node.js Requirements

  • Core environment for TypeScript
  • Recommended version: 24.11+
  • Provides the runtime engine
  • Check version in terminal:
node -v
Ensure your environment is up to date. Node provides the V8 engine necessary to run code outside the browser.

NPM vs NPX

  • NPM: Node Package Manager
  • Used for installing packages
  • NPX: Node Package Execute
  • Runs tools without global installation
This is a critical distinction. We use NPM to download and NPX to run the compiler locally.

Avoid Global Installations

  • Install TS as dev-dependency
  • Prevents version conflicts between projects
  • Ensures project portability
  • Best practice for modern teams
Installing globally can break older projects. Always install TypeScript locally per project.

Initializing Node Projects

  • Create a package.json file
  • Defines project metadata and dependencies
  • Use the -y flag for defaults
npm init -y
This command transitions a standard directory into a functional Node.js project.

Installing TypeScript

  • Add to development dependencies
  • Never edit package-lock.json manually
  • Command:
npm install --save-dev typescript
The --save-dev flag ensures TypeScript is only used during development, not in the final production build.

The TypeScript Compiler (TSC)

  • The "brain" of the process
  • Converts .ts into .js
  • Initialized via NPX
npx tsc --init
Running this command creates the tsconfig.json file, which controls how your code is transformed.

Configuring tsconfig.json

  • Dictates compiler behavior
  • Manages output file generation
  • Key setting: sourceMap
  • Toggle false for clean output
The tsconfig file is highly customizable. We often disable source maps to keep our folders from getting cluttered with .map files.

Cleaning the Output

  • Source maps link TS to JS
  • Useful for debugging
  • Can clutter the directory
  • Disable in tsconfig.json for simplicity
While useful for complex apps, beginners often prefer a clean directory with just .ts and .js files.

The Transpilation Cycle

  • 1. Write TypeScript (.ts)
  • 2. Compile to JavaScript (.js)
  • 3. Run JavaScript with Node
This three-step rhythm is the standard workflow for every TypeScript developer.

Compiling Your Code

  • Invoke the TSC tool
  • Reads tsconfig.json settings
  • Generates the .js equivalent
npx tsc
If you forget to use NPX, you might see a 'command not found' error if TS isn't installed globally.

Running the Result

  • Execute the generated file
  • Use the Node runtime
  • Target the .js file, not .ts
node index.js
Remember: Node only understands the JavaScript file created by the compiler.

Common Gotchas

  • Running 'node index.ts' fails
  • Forgetting NPX prefix
  • Unintended .map file generation
  • Outdated VS Code tooling
Always check your file extensions and ensure VS Code is updated for the best IntelliSense support.

Summary

  • TS is a typed JS superset
  • Install TS locally via NPM
  • Use NPX to run TSC
  • TS is for development only
  • Node runs the compiled JS
The key takeaway is that TypeScript makes writing code safer, but JavaScript is what actually executes.
" >" >
